title: Long-Term Investors, Demand Shifts, and Yields
abstract: I exploit a Dutch reform in the regulatory discount curve that makes the liabilities of pension funds and insurance companies (P&Is) more sensitive to changes in 20-year interest rates but less so to longer maturity rates. Following the reform, P&Is reduced their longest maturity bond holdings but increased those with 20-year maturities, steepening the long end of the yield curve. Using the reform as a shock to identify price elasticities of demand at the sector level based on holdings across maturity buckets and time, I show that banks are more price elastic than other investors and absorb demand shocks.

author.Kristy A E Jansen*: Marshall School of Business, University of Southern California,United States

Recent literature shows that long-term investors, such as pension funds and insurance companies, affect yields (e.g.,Greenwood and Vayanos 2010;Domanski, Shin, and Sushko 2017;Greenwood and Vissing-Jorgensen 2018;Klinger and Sundaresan 2019). The findings in this literature are consistent with the preferred habitat view: demand for specific maturities creates price pressure on bond markets. Demand shocks by these investors can affect yields in case of inelastic demand (e.g.,Koijen and Yogo 2019) that, for instance, arises in the presence of limits to arbitrage (e.g.,Vayanos and Vila 2021).

Because of data limitations, the literature so far has primarily used only price data to study the implications of the preferred habitat view on yields. As a result, two important questions have been left largely unanswered. First, how important are demand shocks from long-term investorsquantitativelyin affecting bond yields? Second, what is themechanismthrough which demand affects yields, and, more importantly, what does that tell us about the arbitrageurs in government bond markets?

To address these questions, I focus on a regulatory reform targeted at pension funds and insurance companies (henceforth: P&Is) in the Netherlands. AsGreenwood and Vissing-Jorgensen (2018)point out, the reform affected long-term bond yields around the announcement day. My contribution is to study the underlying quantities to test and quantify the causal effect of demand shifts on government bond yields. More importantly, the unexpected nature of the reform allows for the identification of price elasticities of demand for several investor types. These demand elasticities give insight in the mechanism behind the observed yield effects because they show which investors respond more to changes in yields, thereby shedding light on the question of who are the arbitrageurs in government bond markets.

The Dutch P&I market is a useful laboratory to study the demand for long-dated assets and its effects on yields for three main reasons. First, the occurrence of a regulatory reform facilitates the clean identification of demand shifts and their effect on yields. The second reason is the availability of detailed data on bond and derivative positions at the institutional level on the one hand, and data about the investorsÔÇÖ liabilities on the other hand. While data on bond holdings are widely available for various investor types, detailed data on derivative positions and liabilities are typically scarce.1Yet, P&Is hedge a substantial amount of their interest rate risk with derivatives. To accurately estimate their demand for long-dated assets, one must have data on both bond and derivative positions. To explain the drivers of demand for long-dated assets, data on the liability structure of P&Is is also key. Third, the Dutch P&I market is economically important because the pension sector alone already covers 58% of the total assets of pension funds in the euro areaOECD 2020.2

I exploit a change in the regulatory discount curve to value liabilities that the Dutch regulator announced and directly implemented on July 2, 2012. This reform altered the way the discount curve is calculated: it now reliesmoreon the 20-year interest rate andlesson rates for longer maturities. For maturities up to 20 years, the curve uses market interest rates. For those over 20 years, it uses a weighted average of the 20-year rate and a fixed rate called the ultimate forward rate (UFR), which is higher than market interest rates. As such, the reform lowered the calculated value of the liabilities. At the same time, liabilities became more sensitive to changes in the 20-year rate, but less sensitive to changes in longer maturity rates.

Why does a change in the regulatory discount curve affect demand for long-dated assets? This demand arises for two reasons: economic and regulatory hedging incentives. The long-term nature of liabilities creates a natural preference for long-term bonds from a liability hedging perspectiveSharpe and Tint 1990;Campbell and Viceira 2002. Regulatory hedging incentives are important when the regulatory framework diverges from the economic state (e.g.,Koijen and Yogo 2015,2016;Sen 2023), because the regulator uses the regulatory discount curve to assess the solvency positions of P&Is. In turn, solvency positions determine the amount pension funds can pay out to retirees or insurance companies to shareholders in terms of dividends. As a result, incentives to hedge the regulatory discount curve rise if the curve diverges from the economic discount curve.

I report the following three main findings. First, consistent with the predictions of a mean-variance framework in an asset-liability context with regulatory constraints, I observe that P&Is with substantial exposure to the regulatory reform, that is, those with long-term liabilities, significantly reduced their bond holdings with a remaining time-to-maturity of at least 30 years (henceforth for brevity: 30-year bond holdings). The aggregate decrease in 30-year bond holdings is economically large: the total decline in demand equals 30% relative to the pre-reform 30-year bond holdings. At the same time, P&Is increase their aggregate bond holdings with maturities close to 20 years by 19% relative to the pre-reform holdings. Both effects are stronger for P&Is that are closer to their solvency constraints, because they have a stronger incentive to hedge the regulatory rather than the economic discount curve. Additionally, I find a decline in the implied duration of swap portfolios after the reform and I show large exposures to the 20-year swap rate but small exposures to longer maturities based on detailed data on swap positions that became available as part of the European Market Infrastructure Regulation (EMIR) in 2017. Finally, the direct capital relief from the reduction in the liability values led to an increase in their allocation to equities and high-yield government bonds. Overall, the results show that the regulatory reform led to a reduction in economic hedges of interest rate risk and to a rise in risky asset exposures at the same time.3

Second, I show that the decreased demand for 30-year bonds and increased demand for 20-year bonds led to a steepening of the long-end of the Dutch yield curve. Because P&Is hold a large share of the Dutch government debt outstanding, I am able to identify the mechanism behind these yield curve effects. To achieve this goal, I estimate demand curves as inKoijen and Yogo (2019)for the other institutions that hold Dutch debt. I use the regulatory reform as an instrument for bond yields, because the regulatory reform created an exogenous demand shift by the Dutch P&I sector that only affected the demand of other investor types through its effect on prices. I justify the plausibly exogenous nature of the reform by the fact that regulator announced and directly implemented the reform 3.5 years before it became part of the new Solvency II regulatory framework in 2016. Even though the discussions at the time evolved around the convergence of regulatory discount rates to a stable level and P&Is may have anticipated the regulatory reform as a result, they did not know the implementation date and the determinants of the shape of the UFR, such as its level and slope. As such, how the reform would affect different maturities is unclear ex ante and in particular the ex post reliance of the curve on the 20-year market interest rate. The less anticipated nature of the reform becomes evident by looking at the sudden increase in the 30-20 year yield spread on the announcement and implementation date of the UFR inFigure┬á1. The unexpected nature of the reform to the market is further motivated by the regulator prohibiting the P&I sector to trade on information discussed during meetings in the weeks leading up to the reform.4That said, to the extent that P&Is anticipated a lower sensitivity of their liabilities to very long-term interest rates, the estimated changes in yields may understate the true effects and demand elasticities provide an upper bound of the true elasticities.

The demand elasticities that I estimate are at the sector level, using data across maturities and across time. I find that banks are most price elastic and largely responsible for absorbing the demand shock. Indeed, after the reform, banks increased their 30-year bond holdings by 18% of the total amount outstanding (TAO) while at the same time they reduced their holdings toward 20-year ones by 9% of the TAO. To better understand why banks absorb demand shocks, I show that P&Is predominantly trade swaps with banks as the counterparty. Moreover, banks hold the opposite exposures compared to P&Is in the swap market. These findings suggest that banksÔÇÖ detailed information about demand for long-dated assets by the P&I sector allows them to quickly react to uninformed demand shocks from this sector.

In response to the reform, P&Is rebalanced not only across Dutch government bonds but also across other safe European bonds, primarily those of France and Germany. As a result, the reform also led to a steepening of the long-end of the yield curve in these countries, although the magnitude of this effect was only half of that observed in the Netherlands. The fact that the reform had a much stronger impact on Dutch government bond markets suggests that the Dutch market is segmented from other safe European bond markets, as otherwise, the yields in various safe European bond markets would move in lockstep in response to the reform. Consistent with this observation, I find similar price elasticities of demand whether I explicitly allow investors to operate only in the Dutch government bond market or when I allow them to substitute between Dutch and other safe European bond markets.

Finally, to generalize my findings, I provide suggestive evidence for the effects of the regulatory reform on yields at the European level. The UFR is an important aspect of the EU Solvency II regulation for insurers in Europe that was announced in August 2015 and took effect on January 1, 2016. Relying on the home-bias found in euro area investorsÔÇÖ portfolios (e.g.,Koijen et┬áal. 2017,2021), I investigate the effects of the UFR on a broad panel of 20 European countries and define the demand for long-term bonds by the size of the insurance sector relative to the total debt outstanding of a country. My estimates suggest that for countries with a small insurance sector changes in yields were negligible; while those with large insurance sectors experienced increases in the 30-20 year yield spread that are of similar magnitude to the Netherlands. Using holdings data at the insurer-country level in the euro area, I confirm that insurance companies substantially increased their exposure to 20-year bonds after Solvency II, consistent with the observed increase in the 30-20 year yield spreads.

My findings have important policy implications. First, they show that the regulatory framework of investors has direct consequences for the governmentsÔÇÖ costs of borrowing. Second, they show that the regulation of long-term investors spills over to the banking sector. As pointed out byKoijen and Yogo (2022), regulation of one sector is typically studied in isolation from other sectors by regulators. My findings, however, suggest that regulators should take the regulatory framework of banks into account when analyzing the introduction of new regulatory policies targeted at long-term investors.

This study contributes to the preferred habitat theoryCulbertson 1957;Modigliani and Sutch 1966;Vayanos and Vila 2021that posits that some investors prefer specific maturities and that their demand for bonds with those maturities influences the interest rates at those maturities.Greenwood and Vayanos (2014)find that supply shocks affect yields because they change the duration risk of arbitrageurs. Similarly,Guibaud, Nosbusch, and Vayanos (2013)show that an increase in investors with a longer investment horizon renders long-term bonds more expensive and increases their optimal supply by the government. I contribute to this literature by providing direct evidence in favor of the preferred habitat theory.

My findings also contribute to the work that empirically tests the implications of the preferred habitat or the demand-based view. For instance,Domanski, Shin, and Sushko (2017)argue that the ÔÇ£hunt-for-durationÔÇØ of insurance companies might have amplified the decline in bond yields in the euro area. Similarly,Klinger and Sundaresan (2019)explain that the negative 30-year U.S. swap spreads are the result of underfunded pension plans optimally using swaps for duration hedging. Additionally,Greenwood and Vayanos (2010)show that changes in regulation which increase the incentive to buy (sell) long-term bonds result in a decline (increase) in long-term yields around policy announcement days.5Closest to my paper,Greenwood and Vissing-Jorgensen (2018)also use the regulatory reform that is studied here to investigate the impact of P&I demand on bond yields. The key contribution to their work is that I bring in quantities which allow me to explicitly test and quantify the causal impact of hedging demand on government bond yields. I further complement their work by using the reform to back out the aggregate and sector-specific price elasticities of demand, shedding light on the market segmentation in government bond markets.

My study also contributes to the growing demand-based asset pricing literatureKoijen and Yogo 2019.Koijen et┬áal. (2017,2021) apply this model to study the effects of quantitative easing (QE) on yields in the euro area andBretscher et┬áal. (2024)study the implications of inelastic demand for corporate bond pricing. A key challenge in demand-based asset pricing is to find clean instruments for prices. I make progress in this direction by using an exogenous demand shift as an instrument to establish a causal link between demand and asset prices. Compared to the traditional instrument inKoijen and Yogo (2019)that is based on unobservable investment mandates, I use an instrument that is empirically observable. Furthermore, I employ an instrument that is largely free from anticipation effects, unlike for instance, the capital key instrument used inKoijen et┬áal. (2021)that was known in advance of the QE programs.

While recent literature in demand-based asset pricing directly incorporates detailed portfolio holdings to understand asset prices, earlier works have recognized the price impact of (institutional) investor demand. For instance,Wermers (1999),Nofsinger and Sias (1999),Gompers and Metrick (2001), andCohen, Gompers, and Vuolteenaho (2002), among others, demonstrate that stock prices rise (fall) with large institutional purchases (sales). In currency markets, studies byCai et┬áal. (2001),Evans and Lyons (2002), andFroot and Ramadorai (2005), among others, have linked large investor flows to transitory exchange rate dynamics. Likewise, in the international real estate market, research byBadarinza and Ramadorai (2018),Gorback and Keys (2020), andFavilukis and Van Nieuwerburgh (2021)document that foreign demand influences real estate prices. While these studies highlight price impact of investor demand, the underlying mechanisms remain largely unexplored. My paper addresses this gap by considering the demand from all investors simultaneously to understand the behavior of investors that ultimately have to absorb these large demand shocks. My framework therefore provides a useful tool for understanding cross-sectional variation in price impact across markets and asset classes.

Additionally, my study links to the intermediary asset pricing literature, which directly models intermediaries and how they matter for asset prices (e.g.,He and Krishnamurthy 2013;Adrian, Etula, and Muir 2014;Haddad and Muir 2021;Baron and Muir 2022). The focus in these studies is on the link between asset prices and constraints of (investment) banks, hedge funds, and broker dealers. Instead, I provide evidence for the importance of regulatory constraints for the prime holders of government debt: the P&I sector.

Finally, my findings link to the literature on the effects of regulation for the investment and hedging behavior of long-term investors.Ellul, Jotikasthira, and Lundblad (2011)show that insurance companiesÔÇÖ regulatory constraints induce fire sales;Becker and Ivashina (2015)find that insurers are biased toward high-yielding bonds conditional on credit ratings;Becker, Opp, and Saidi (2022)show that insurance companies sold fewer poorly rated MBS after elimination of capital requirements on MBS; andEllul et┬áal. (2022)find that variable annuities induce more risky and illiquid bond holdings.Andonov, Bauer, and Cremers (2017)show that U.S. public pension plans invest riskier to report a better funding status. Closest to my paper isSen (2023), who finds that insurance companies only hedge interest rate risk and equity risk when the regulatory framework recognizes such risks. My findings also show that regulatory incentives are key in shaping the hedging decisions of P&Is.
In this section, I describe the regulatory framework of both pension funds and insurance companies and explain the regulatory reform in detail.

1.1 Regulatory framework P&Is

Pension funds and insurance companies are regulated by the Dutch Central Bank (DNB) and in this section I briefly describe both regulatory frameworks. For both sectors, it is important to note that the valuation of assets has been mark-to-market since 2007.6

A pension fundÔÇÖs solvency position is assessed by computing its funding ratio, or the assets divided by the liabilities. A pension fundÔÇÖs required funding ratio is based on a pension fundÔÇÖs risk profile and is calculated such that the probability that capital (the funding ratio) falls below zero (100%) on a 1-year horizon equals 2.5%. In case a pension fund is not compliant with the required funding ratio, it files a recovery plan to the supervisor. Additionally, pension funds are not allowed to index pension rights if the funding ratio falls below a minimum threshold of 104.2%. If the funding ratio falls below 90% a reduction in accrued benefits may be required. Note that the regulatory framework of Dutch pension funds is very different from its U.S. counterpart, where risk-based capital requirements are absentBoon, Bri├¿re, and Rigot 2018.

An insurance companyÔÇÖs solvency position is assessed by computing its solvency ratio, or the available capital divided by the required capital. Prior to Solvency II, the solvency ratios of insurance companies were not risk-based and the DNB required capital to equal 4% of the value of the liabilities. At the introduction of Solvency II, it required capital to be computed like for pension funds, except that it calculated required capital such that the probability of capital falling below zero on a 1-year horizon equaled 0.5%, rather than the 2.5% for pension funds. In case an insurance company is not compliant with the required solvency requirements, it also files a recovery plan to the supervisor. Dividend policies are typically based on internal target solvency ratios. For instance, Allianz only pays out dividends if the solvency ratio exceeds 160%.7

1.2 Regulatory discount curve

The regulator uses the regulatory discount curve to estimate the liability value and hence the solvency positions of P&Is. Prior to the start of July 2012, the DNB based the regulatory discount curve on the euro swap curve for all maturities and used market interest rates up to a maturity of 50 years. Afterward, it extrapolated the interest rates for maturities beyond 50 years from the last observed forward rate. Because it based the regulatory discount curve on market interest rates only, the curve equaled the economic discount curve. The DNB announced and directly implemented a change in the regulatory discount curve on July 2, 2012 in anticipation of the EU introducing its Solvency II regulatory framework for insurance companies in 2016. The new curve is similar to the regulatory discount curve in Solvency II that would become applicable to all European insurers. The DNB announced a similar regulatory discount curve for pension funds on September 24, 2012.8

The DNBÔÇÖs new regulatory discount curve uses an extrapolation method based on the ultimate forward rate (UFR) that is the convergence of long interest rates to a stable level. In essence, this new curve uses market interest rates up to a maturity of 20 years, and the DNB determines interest rates with maturities longer than 20 years by using a weighted average between the market interest rates and a fixed rate, the UFR. The main argument to justify the implementation of the UFR was that the market for long durations is less liquid and only a few securities with such long durations existed. As a result, the DNB regarded the implied market interest rates as unreliable: a discount curve purely based on market data was highly sensitive to liquidity shocks and therefore also the solvency positions of P&Is. A regulatory discount curve based on the UFR solved this issue by making the long-end of the curve less dependent on market interest rates.9

The euro swap rates for maturities of 1 to 10, 12, 15, and 20 years are converted to zero-coupon interest rates by means of bootstrapping. For nonobservable swap rates, the DNB estimates zero interest rates by assuming constant forward rates.Forward rates exceeding 20 year maturities are a weighted average between the 20-year forward rate and the UFR. The weight increases linearly in maturity, and the level of the UFR equals 4.2%. The regulatory discount curve with the UFR has three important effects. First, the UFR decreases current liability values as liabilities are discounted against higher rates. Second, the UFR makes the regulatory discount curve less sensitive to parallel shifts in interest rate changes for maturities beyond 20 years. Panel A ofFigure┬á2displays these two effects. Third, the UFR results in a higher exposure to changes in the 20-year interest rate as shown in panel B ofFigure┬á2. article.2 Model Intuition:
To better micro-found how P&Is changed their asset demand following the reform, I derive my main testable predictions from a simple partial equilibrium mean-variance optimization framework with assets and liabilities. The key driver of my predictions is the assumption that P&Is care about both their economic and regulatory solvency positions. The full version of the model is inInternet Appendix IA4, but I give a summary here to get the main intuition across.

For the assets, I assume that P&Is can invest in a risky assetSwith corresponding returnrt+1SÔüá, and in a set of safe European bondsB(h) with corresponding maturityhand returnrt+1(hÔêÆ1)Ôüá. The vector of bond returns is denoted byrt+1BÔüá. I assume that the yield curve can be determined using this set of bonds. The risk-free rate is denoted byrfand total assets byAt. The assets therefore evolve asAt+1=(1+rf+wtS(rt+1SÔêÆrf)+wtBÔÇ▓(rt+1BÔêÆrf1))AtÔüá.

Throughout, the simplifying assumption is that P&Is consider all safe European bonds as close substitutes and therefore the only relevant dimension is maturity. This is a simplification because P&Is hold safe European bonds from various countries even though their yields diverge. If P&Is consider these bonds to be perfect substitutes, then P&Is would only hold (buy) those safe European bonds with the highest yields (lowest prices). Even though this is a simplifying assumption, the object of study is thechangein holdings following the reform. The underlying assumption, therefore, is that P&Is do not discriminate across countries in response to the reform. This is a plausible assumption because within the regulatory framework, safe European government bonds are treated in the same way. That is, hedging one unit with a bond from one safe European country versus another has the same impact on the regulatory solvency position. In the next section, I confirm that P&Is rebalanced in a similar way within various safe European bond markets in response to the reform.

For the liabilities, I assume that P&Is have to pay out a fixed and promised set of cash flows for each maturityh,CF(h)Ôüá.12The return on the liabilities is denoted byrt+1LÔüá. Because of the promised nature of the cash flows, the economic value of the liabilities is determined using market interest rates, and hencert+1L=rt+1BÔüá. The economic value of the liabilities therefore evolves asLEt+1=atÔÇ▓(1+rt+1B)LEtÔüá, whereatis a vector that contains the cash flow distribution of the liabilities. Intuitively,ait(h)is the fraction of the total liabilities that have to be paid inhyears from now.

The regulatory value of the liabilities diverges from its economic counterpart in that its sensitivity to market interest rates is different. The regulatory value of the liabilities evolves asLRt+1=aÔÇ▓t(1+╬¥ÔÇ▓Lrt+1B)LRtÔüá, where the sensitivity of the regulatory discount curve to market interest rates is defined by╬¥Land has the same length as the set of bonds. This sensitivity means that the economic and regulatory value of the liabilities are identical if╬¥L(h)=1for all maturitiesh, which was the case prior to the reform. On the other hand, the regulatory value of the liabilities is insensitive to changes in the interest rate if╬¥L(h)=0for all maturitiesh. An example is a regulatory discount curve that is based on a fixed rate for all maturities. The reform implied that╬¥L(h)became larger than one for maturities close to 20 years, equivalent to an increased exposure of the regulatory liabilities to the 20-year market interest rate. At the same time,╬¥L(h)converges to zero for 30-year maturities, or a reduced exposure of the regulatory liabilities to the 30-year market interest rate.

The optimal demand for the risky asset consists of speculative demand only, because the liabilities are valued using the yield curve, and the safe bonds are assumed to be independent of the risky asset. The bond portfolio consists of three components: the speculative demand, the economic hedging demand, and the regulatory hedging demand. The economic (regulatory) hedging demand equals the desired hedge against changes in the economic (regulatory) liability value. The heterogeneity in demand for long-term bonds across P&Is depends on two main factors. First, the demand for long-term bonds depends on the distribution of the cash flow payments: if many of the liabilities are due in 30 years, that is, a highat(30)Ôüá, the hedging demand for 30-year bonds is higher, all else equal. Second, the demand for long-term bonds depends on the weight assigned to the economic versus regulatory hedging demand. This weight depends on the relative magnitudes of╬╗(FRt)and╬│that is driven by solvency positionsFRtÔüá: the closer P&Is are to the regulatory constraint, the stronger the desire to hedge the regulatory funding ratio and hence a higher weight is assigned to the regulatory hedging portfolio. For instance, if a large weight is assigned to the regulatory hedging portfolio (large╬╗(FRt)Ôüá) and the regulatory discount curve is insensitive to changes in 30-year market interest rates, that is,╬¥(30)=0Ôüá, the total demand for 30-year bonds approaches zero.

InInternet Appendix IA4, I formally derive the model implications of the regulatory reform by taking the difference between the model-implied optimal demand after and before the reform. This results in the following model implications:

Prediction 1 - P&Is with long liability durations reduce their 30-year bond holdings and increase those with 20-year maturities more compared to P&Is with short liability durations. Intuitively, the first prediction simply states that P&Is with long liability durations, so those that pay out in the far future, are more affected by the reform and hence have a stronger incentive to move away from 30-year bond holdings toward 20-year ones.

Prediction 2 - P&Is with long liability durations increase their risky asset holdings more compared to P&Is with short liability durations. P&Is with long liability durations saw a sharper decline in their liability values and hence experienced the largest capital relief. Because ╬╗ ( Ôïà ) is a convex and decreasing function of the regulatory funding ratio, those P&Is have the strongest incentive to increase their speculative portfolio.

Prediction 3 - P&Is close to their solvency constraint reduce their 30-year bond holdings and increase those with 20-year maturities more compared to unconstrained P&Is. The more constrained investors are, the stronger the incentive to hedge the regulatory discount curve which implies moving away from 30-year bonds to those with 20-year maturities. In Internet Appendix IA4 , I close the model by introducing an arbitrageur and impose market clearing to study the model-implied effects on the yield curve Vayanos and Vila 2021 . I then calibrate the model and show that the model-implied yield curve steepened because of the reform in Figure IA3 . The steepening of the model-implied yield curve corresponds to the observed change in the yield curve around the implementation date of the reform as shown in Figure IA4 .
article.3 Data:
In this section, I describe the data sources that I use for my analysis (Subsection 3.1) and provide summary statistics for the sample (Subsection 3.2).

3.1 Constructing the data set

I use data on Securities Holdings Statistics (SHS) for insurance companies and pension funds in the Netherlands over the period 2009q1-2019q4. All institutions that report are domiciled in the Netherlands and the regulator decides which institutions have to report in order to have sufficient coverage in terms of AUM for every sector. Institutions have to report their holdings of all securities, both foreign and domestic, to the regulator on a quarterly basis.14DNB gathers holdings data to compute, among other things, the Dutch balance of payments, international investment positions, and the financial accounts.15

The data provide bond and stock holdings with International Securities Identification Numbers (ISIN). The holdings of stocks are available in the number of shares and market values. The holdings of bonds are available in both nominal and market values. The SHS database is then linked to the Centralised Securities Database (CSDB). The aim of this database is to hold accurate information on all individual securities relevant for the statistical purposes of the European System of Central Banks,ECB (2010). I obtain the following relevant market information from the CSDB database: debt type, maturity dates, coupon rates, coupon frequencies, coupon type (e.g., fixed, floating or zero-coupon), last coupon payment date, yield-to-maturity (YTM), prices, and total amount outstanding. I compute bond durations using YTMs and measure credit risk for corporate bonds using the distance to default (DTD) available from the Credit Research Initiative at the National University of Singapore. Credit risk for government bonds is measured using credit ratings from Fitch.

Next, I link the holdings data to the supervision databases. The supervision databases are from mandatory annual and quarterly statements that P&Is report to the DNB. P&Is have to report, among other things, solvency positions, liability durations, and asset allocations as well as the value of the assets and liabilities. For pension funds, I take the reported funding ratios directly from the files. For insurance companies, I convert the solvency ratios to funding ratios, because the model makes predictions based on funding ratios.16Formally, prior to Solvency II, solvency ratios equaledSR=AÔêÆL0.04Lwhich meant that the funding ratio equaledAL=0.04ÔêùSR+1Ôüá. The solvency ratios under Solvency II are more complex, and hence I hand-collect data on the assets and the liabilities for each insurer to compute the funding ratios manually.

3.2 The sample

The total sample covers 42 pension funds, 12 life insurers, and 27 nonlife insurers. This group of institutional investors represents around 80%-90% of the AUM for all institutional investors domiciled in the Netherlands.

I only analyze investorsÔÇÖ direct holdings, that is, investments that are not made via other investor types such as mutual funds. The data, unfortunately, do not allow for a linkage between the indirect holdings of investors to their direct holdings, except for the two largest pension funds and the two largest insurance companies. For these P&Is, I know their shares in mutual funds so I can use the reported holdings of mutual funds to obtain their indirect holdings.Table┬á1shows that after correcting the holdings for these four P&Is, the fraction of assets that are incorporated in my analysis equals on average 85% of the total AUM (direct+indirect).

article.4 Empirical Methodology:
I turn to the main tests of the empirical predictions from my theoretical framework next. For bond holdings, I use thenotionalamounts in all my analyses such that I capture active choices by investors and market prices are not driving the results. Moreover, in my main analysis, I focus on maturity buckets to take account of the fact that bonds with similar maturities are close substitutes. I therefore refer to bonds with maturities well above 20 years as ÔÇ£30-year bondsÔÇØ that are defined as bonds with a remaining time-to-maturity of at least 30 years. I define bonds with maturities close to 20 years as those with remaining times to maturities between 15 and 25 years.

4.1 Long-term bond holdings and the regulatory reform

Panel A ofFigure┬á3shows the average fraction of P&IsÔÇÖ total bond portfolio in 30-year bonds. Likewise, panel B shows the fraction of the bond portfolio with maturities close to 20 years.19Both graphs split the sample into P&Is with long (above the median) and short (below the median) liability durations. While both groups show similar trends prior to the reform, the P&Is with long liability durations sharply reduced their allocation to 30-year bonds when the regulatory reform was announced (and implemented). The exact opposite pattern is visible for the allocation toward 20-year bonds, with a sudden and sharp increase in its allocation. Over the sample period, the allocation to 30-year (20-year) bonds remains substantially lower (higher) than the pre-UFR levels.

To align the predictions of the model with the data, I use a difference-in-difference specification which compares long-term bond holdings before and after the implementation of the UFR: w i t B = ╬▒ + ╬▓ 1 D 2011 q 2 , i L ├ù UFR t + ╬▓ 2 FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 + ╬▓ 3 FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 ├ù PF i + ╬▓ 4 D i t ÔêÆ 1 L + ╬▓ 5 AUM i t ÔêÆ 1 + ╬▓ 6 Vol FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 + ╬¢ i + ╬╗ t + ¤Á i t , (6) where w i t B is the bond allocation of P&I i at time t ; 20 UFR t equals one as of the announcement (and implementation) day of the UFR and zero otherwise; D 2011 q 2 , i L is a time-invariant characteristic that equals the liability duration as of 2011q2; FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 is the lagged inverse of the funding ratio minus one; PF i is a dummy variable that equals one if the investor is a pension fund; D i t ÔêÆ 1 L is the lagged liability duration; AUM i t ÔêÆ 1 is the lagged total AUM; Vol FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 is the lagged annual volatility of the inverse of the funding ratio; ╬¢ i is the fund fixed effects; and ╬╗ t is the time fixed effects.

I use the liability duration as of 2011q2 in the interaction term to ensure that the liabilities are not in any way affected by the regulatory reform, while at the same time ensuring representativeness of the liabilities as of the regulatory reform. An inverse of the funding ratio equal to one means that P&Is are neither overfunded nor underfunded. Therefore, subtracting one from the inverse of the funding ratio has the advantage that the coefficient is easy to interpret with values above zero indicating underfunding. The regression specification also allows for differences in responses to a decline in funding positions across pension funds and insurers. Although size does not appear in the model, it is added as a control because empirical studies have shown that size is an important driver of investment decisions (e.g.,Pollet and Wilson 2008). Finally, the volatility of the regulatory funding ratio is added as control because the funding ratio alone may not be sufficient to control for P&Is regulatory constraints. The reason is that P&Is that are well-funded but risky may not necessarily be less constrained than P&Is that are less funded but maintain a safer investment strategy.

Table┬á2summarizes the results. P&Is with long liability durations decrease 30-year holdings to a larger extent than the ones with short liability durations. At the same time, P&Is increased their bond holdings with maturities varying between 15 and 25 years, while they did not change their holdings of bonds with maturities fewer than 15 years. These results support the first prediction of my theoretical framework in Section 2.

The effects are also economically significant. The total decline in 30-year bond holdings approximately equalsÔêæi=1N╬▓1^├ùD2011q2,iL├ùAUM2012q1,iB=Ôé¼10.15 billion, whereAUM2012q1,iBis the total AUM in bonds (nominal terms) for P&Iiin the two quarters before the regulatory reform was announced and implemented in 2012q3. The decline is equivalent to a decrease of 33% relative to the pre-reform 30-year bond holdings. Similarly, P&Is increased their 20-year bond holdings by Ôé¼15.71 billion, or 17% relative to the pre-reform 20-year bond holdings.

The regulatory discount curve is based on the euro swap curve, and hence the suitable interest rates to replicate liabilities are ÔÇ£risk-freeÔÇØ euro interest rates. From a regulatory perspective, the countries that are considered credit-risk free in the regulatory framework are those countries with AAA ratings and that are denominated in euro.21Around the reform, the following euro area countries had AAA ratings and made up the lion share of P&IsÔÇÖ government bond portfolios: France, Germany, and the Netherlands (Table IA1 of the Internet Appendix).22Table IA2 of the Internet Appendixreplicates the results for Dutch, French, and German debt only. As expected by focusing on safe euro area government debt, the rebalancing effects are substantially stronger in economic magnitude.

To give additional support for the economic effects, I have also reestimated the regression based on Dutch government bond holdings alone (Table IA3 of the Internet Appendix). The aggregate decline in 30-year Dutch bond holdings equals Ôé¼2.61 billion. The corresponding amount outstanding equaled Ôé¼12.13 billion at the implementation of the UFR, and hence, the total decline corresponded to 22% of its amount outstanding. Similarly, the aggregate increase in bond holdings with maturities between 15 and 25 years equaled Ôé¼4.94 billion, or 26% of its amount outstanding.

My model also predicts that P&Is with long liability durations allocate more to risky assets after implementation of the UFR compared to those with short liability durations. To capture changes in risky asset allocations, I simultaneously study the asset allocation dynamics of stocks, corporate bonds, and government bonds. For corporate and government bonds, I also analyze the evolution of credit risk within both asset classes to distinguish between high risk or illiquid bonds Ellul et┬áal. 2022 . Then, I use the following difference-in-difference specification to formally test the hypothesis: w i t S = ╬▒ + ╬▓ 1 D 2011 q 2 , i L ├ù UFR t + ╬▓ 2 FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 + ╬▓ 3 FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 ├ù PF i + ╬▓ 4 D i t ÔêÆ 1 L + ╬▓ 5 AUM i t ÔêÆ 1 + ╬▓ 6 Vol FR i t ÔêÆ 1 ÔêÆ 1 + ╬¢ i + ╬╗ t + ¤Á i t , (7) where w i t S is the risky asset allocation of P&I i at time t . 23

Table┬á3shows the results. The first column confirms that P&Is with long liability durations increase their equity allocation to a larger extent than the ones with short liability durations. A one-standard-deviation increase in the liability duration (6.49) expands the equity allocation by 1.49% but reduces the corporate bond allocation by 1.14%. The allocation to government bonds remains unaltered. Within corporate bonds, there is no evidence of a change in credit risk. On the other hand, a longer liability duration increases the fraction of government bonds allocated to countries with higher credit risk. Column 6 shows that a one-standard-deviation increase in the liability duration increases the fraction of high-yield government bonds held by 1.62%. In sum, these findings show that P&Is move their assets away from corporate bonds to stocks and at the same time increase the riskiness of their government bond portfolios. This finding is consistent withGe and Weisbach (2021)andKoijen and Yogo (2022), who show that tighter constraints, rather than relaxed ones, result in lower allocations to risky assets.

article.5 The Effect of Demand Shifts on Long-Te:
In this section, I estimate the effect of the regulatory reform on Dutch government bond yields by using the construction of the UFR as an exogenous shock to demand that affects yields differently at different maturities. I then use this construction as an instrument to estimate price elasticities of demand for various investor types by using the framework ofKoijen and Yogo (2019). I also show the implications of the reform for yields in other safe euro area countries and study differences between short-term and long-term price elasticities of demand. Finally, I provide suggestive evidence as to why some investor types are more price elastic in the government bond market than others.

Even though the previous section shows that the demand shift from 30-year to 20-year bonds is visible across various (safe) bonds and not specific to Dutch debt, here I focus on Dutch government bond yields. The reason is that the demand shock and corresponding price impact were largest for Dutch debt, which allows for a clean identification of price elasticities of demand. This approach implicitly assumes that other investors only absorb the shock in the Dutch government bond market, and not in other safe European bond markets. This is an extreme assumption, because these investors simultaneously operate in the Dutch and other safe European bond markets. In Section 5.3, however, I show that my results do not materially change when I allow investors to operate in both markets.

5.1 Data

To estimate the demand curves, I extend the Dutch holdings data with the ECB holdings data to obtain a larger coverage of Dutch government bond holders. The euro area Securities Holdings Statistics by Sector (SHSS) data provides the sector-country holdings for each sector and country in the euro area over the period 2009q1-2019q4. Like for the Dutch SHS data, per country-sector, the data contains information on the quarter-end holdings at the ISIN level. For instance, the SHSS data reports the aggregate holdings of German banks in a specific security.24The euro area SHSS data, like the Dutch SHS data, are linked to the CSDB database to obtain market information about the securities held. The five distinct sectors that I incorporate in my analysis are banks, mutual funds, P&I sector (outside the Netherlands), other (households, nonfinancial institutions, etc), and the foreign sector. The investments of the foreign sector are defined as the difference between the total amount outstanding minus the holdings of all the euro area investors combined.

5.2 The connection between portfolio holdings and yields

As shown inFigure┬á1, the 30-20 year government bond spread increased significantly after the announcement and implementation of the UFR, and remained at a higher level thereafter.Figure IA4.2furthermore shows that the observed yield curve moved from being inverted at the longer end to become upward-sloping after the reform.25These findings are consistent with the model-implied yields as laid out briefly in Section 2 and in detail inInternet Appendix IA4.2, where I close the model by introducing an arbitrageur and impose market clearing.

Next to showing the effect on the yield curve, the goal of this section is to provide insight into the mechanism behind the yield effects. So even though the model gives suggestions about the change in the slope of the yield curve, it does not directly speak to the magnitude of the effects nor the investors that are driving it. To get more insight in the mechanism, I estimate the demand curves of the other investors that hold Dutch debt and thus have to absorb the demand shock that is caused by the Dutch P&I sector. Using the demand curves, I can measure their price elasticities of demand which, in turn, allow me to study price effects as well as the importance of various investor types in creating price effects.

To estimate demand curves, I apply the asset demand system developed byKoijen and Yogo (2019). Formally, investoriÔÇÖs investment in Dutch government bonds within maturity buckethis denoted byBit(h)Ôüá, and the investment in the outside asset is denoted byOit.26I cannot observe what investors consider to be the outside asset, so I use the 10-year German yield as a proxy for the outside asset because German government bonds are likely good substitutes for Dutch ones.

The portfolio weight in the framework of Koijen and Yogo (2019) is then defined as: w i t ( h ) = B i t ( h ) O i t + Ôêæ h = 1 H = 7 B i t ( h ) = ╬┤ i t ( h ) 1 + Ôêæ h = 1 H = 7 ╬┤ i t ( h ) , (9) where ╬┤ i t ( h ) = w i t ( h ) w i t ÔêÆ 1 ( 0 ) = B i t ( h ) O i t ÔêÆ 1 and w i t ( 0 ) = 1 ÔêÆ Ôêæ h = 1 H = 7 w i t ( h ) equals the fraction invested in the outside asset. The demand of investor i for government bonds with maturity h is a function of bond yields and characteristics Koijen et┬áal. 2021 : ln Ôüí B i t ( h ) = ln Ôüí ╬┤ i t ( h ) + ln Ôüí O i t = ╬▒ ^ i + ╬▓ 0 i y t ( h ) + ╬▓ 1 i ÔÇ▓ x t ( h ) + ╬▓ ^ 2 i y t D E + ╬▓ 3 i ln Ôüí ( B 2009 q 1 , i ( h ) ) + ¤Á i t ( h ) , (10) in which ╬▒ ^ i = ╬▒ i + ln Ôüí O i , ╬▓ ^ 2 i = ╬▓ 2 i + ¤ê i , y t ( h ) is the average yield for maturity bucket h , x t ( h ) represents bond characteristics, and y t D E is the 10-year German yield and captures alternative investment opportunities outside of the Netherlands. 27 Moreover, the inclusion of the initial holdings, ln Ôüí ( B 2009 q 1 , i ( h ) ) Ôüá , captures time-invariant omitted characteristics.

Koijen and Yogo (2019)show that(10)is consistent with a model in which investors have mean-variance preferences over returns, assume that returns follow a factor model, and assume that both the expected returns and factor loadings are affine in a set of characteristics. The component of demand that is not captured by prices, characteristics, and time-invariant characteristics,¤Áit(h)Ôüá, is referred to as latent demand. The underlying assumption forEquation (10)to hold is that supply is exogenously given or fixed and that other bond characteristics have not changed because of the reform. I provide evidence that both assumptions hold in my setting inInternet Appendix IA8.

Moving to the bond characteristics, I assume that yields are primarily driven by duration and convexity (measured as the duration squared), because Dutch government bonds are regarded as safe (long-term) assets.28I also add the average coupons and the total amount outstanding (TAO) for each maturity bucket as characteristics that drive demand for bonds. Investors who aim to match their cash flows might have a preference for coupon bonds and likewise, others may have a preference for more or less liquid bonds proxied by TAO. For the investors, I aggregate the holdings of all investors within a sector as inKoijen et┬áal. (2021).29

To obtain consistent estimates of the parameters in(10)using ordinary least squares (OLS), one has to assume that characteristics are exogenous to latent demand. However, a positive latent demand for Dutch government bonds of a particular maturity may result in lower yields. The demand curves are therefore estimated using an instrumental variable approach.Koijen and Yogo (2019)propose an instrument that is not ideal in government bond markets. Their instrument uses the investment mandates of other investors to construct an instrument that is related to yields but orthogonal to latent demand. The reason is that the investment universe for government bonds is not as restricted and predetermined as for the equityKoijen and Yogo 2019and corporate bond marketBretscher et┬áal. 2024. Indeed,Table IA5 of the Internet Appendixshows that only 53% of the government bonds that are currently held in the portfolios of euro area investors were in the portfolio the previous quarter.

I therefore take advantage of the regulatory reform and use the weights assigned to the UFR as an instrument for changes in yields. I justify the plausible exogenous nature of the reform as follows. Even though investors may have anticipated the UFR, they did not know the determinants of the shape of the UFR, such as its level and the slope. In fact, in discussions between the regulator and the P&I sector about the UFR in the weeks before its implementation, the regulator prohibited P&Is to trade on this information. One specific insurer, Delta Lloyd, did not comply and as a result, received a Ôé¼22.7 million fine.30As a result, the demand shift by the P&I sector is close to exogenous and can be used to estimate the demand curves of the other investors, because the demand shift only affects holdings of other investors through its effect on prices. To the extent that P&Is anticipated a lower sensitivity of their liabilities to very long-term interest rates, the estimated changes in yields may understate the true effects and the demand elasticities that I estimate provide an upper bound of the true elasticities.

The instrument is constructed in such a way that its negative for the maturity buckets(15,20]and(20,25]because of the excess demand for 20-year bonds, whereas the instrument is positive for the maturity buckets(25,30]and(30,Ôê×)because of the reduced demand for 30-year maturities. Specifically, the instrument is constructed as the average weight assigned to the UFR for each maturity bucket summarized inTable IA12 of the Internet Appendix, minus the total weight assigned to the 20-year interest rate equally distributed over the(15,20]and(20,25]maturity buckets. The instrumental variable is then defined aszt(h)=╬¥(h)UFRtÔüá, where╬¥(h)is the average weight assigned to the UFR for maturity bucketh.

To derive asset pricing effects from the demand system, I can perform a simple back-of-the-envelope calculation. Pension funds and insurers sold 22% of the amount outstanding of 30-year Dutch government bonds. This percentage means a price effect equal to22%/4.31=5.11%Ôüá. For a bond with a maturity of 30 years, this percentage means an increase in 30-year yields of 17 basis points, which is close to the price effect found for the first-stage regression.

5.3 Accounting for other safe euro area government bonds

So far, I have shown that the regulatory reform affected the long-end of the Dutch yield curve. However, P&Is did not only rebalance their Dutch government bond portfolio but also sell 30-year bonds and buy 20-year ones across various fixed income assets. In particular, as shown inInternet Appendix Table IA1, the other two main fixed income assets that P&Is hold are German and French government bonds.Figure IA1 of the Internet Appendixshows that as a fraction of the amount outstanding, P&Is also reduced their 30-year bond holdings in France and Germany.31Consistent with this selling behavior across various safe euro area countries,Greenwood and Vissing-Jorgensen (2018)show that the 30-10 year yield spreads in other safe euro area countries went up by 8 basis points around the announcement date of the reform, which is half the magnitude of the effect on Dutch debt.

The fact that the price effect was substantially larger for Dutch bonds compared to other safe euro area bonds already suggests that markets are segmented. Otherwise the yields in all safe euro area countries would move in lockstep, as any price divergence between different safe euro area bonds would directly be arbitraged away. In my analysis, I implicitly assume an extreme form of market segmentation, because I only allow the other investors to operate in the Dutch market. This is an extreme assumption, because these investors also operate in other safe euro area bond markets. In addition, the fact that Dutch P&Is also moved from 30-year to 20-year bonds in other safe euro area bond markets implies that the other investors had to absorb the demand shock not only in the Dutch debt market, but also in these other safe euro area bond markets.

To incorporate this idea in the demand system, I separate the Dutch yield y t N L ( h ) such that y t N L ( h ) = y t N L , S ( h ) + y t E U ( h ) Ôüá , where y t N L , S ( h ) equals the difference between the Dutch yield and the safe euro yield y t E U ( h ) Ôüá . Specifically, in the demand system we get: ln Ôüí B i t ( h ) = ln Ôüí ╬┤ i t ( h ) + ln Ôüí O i t = ╬▒ ^ i + ╬▓ 0 i y t N L , S ( h ) + ╬▓ 1 i y t E U ( h ) + ╬▓ 2 i ÔÇ▓ x t ( h ) + ╬▓ ^ 3 i y t U S + ╬▓ 4 i ln Ôüí ( B 2009 q 1 , i ( h ) ) + ¤Á i t ( h ) . (13)

In words, demand for Dutch bonds now depends on the safe euro yield(ytEU(h))and the difference between the Dutch and the safe euro yield(ytNL,S(h))Ôüá. In the data, I computeytEU(h)as the value-weighted yield of safe European countries other than the Netherlands. I then compute the Dutch yield spreadytNL,S(h)as the difference between the Dutch yield and the corresponding European counterpart. For the outside asset, I take the 10-year U.S. government bond yield.32

To estimate the demand system, I construct an instrument for bothytNL,S(h)andytEU(h)Ôüá. I cannot use the regulatory reform directly, because it is not country-specific. Instead, I focus on an alternative instrument that uses the high-frequency changes in yields around the event day. Specifically, the instrument forytEU(h)equals the value-weighted changes in yields in safe European countries, except for the Netherlands, in a 1-day window around the announcement and implementation day of the regulatory reform (Ôüá╬¥EU(h)inTable IA6). The instrument forytNL,S(h)is the difference in the yield changes observed in the Netherlands, and those changes observed for the value-weighted safe European bond portfolio in the same 1-day window (the difference between╬¥NL(h)and╬¥EU(h)inTable IA6). The instrument satisfies the exclusion restriction under the assumption that the regulatory reform was the only event affecting safe long-term yields around the event day. To be conservative, I therefore focus on 1-day yield changes (rather than lower frequencies).

Notice that conceptually, this approach is very similar toChaudhary, Fu, and Li (2023), who use a bondÔÇÖs own idiosyncratic mutual fund flow shocks to back out the price multiplier of the bondÔÇÖs own demand shock, while they use the value-weighted idiosyncratic mutual fund flow shocks of a substitute portfolio of other, similar bonds to control for the substitute portfolio. Translating this to my setting, when we observe a demand shock to the Dutch portfolio, it is plausible that other similar bonds may also experience demand shocks, which is consistent with the finding in the first part of the paper that Dutch P&Is sell not only Dutch bonds but also other safe European bonds.

Table A1summarizes the results. The demand elasticities with respect to the Dutch yield spread are close to the previous estimates of investorsÔÇÖ sensitivities to the Dutch yield without controlling for the substitute portfolio. Reestimating the price elasticities of demand inTable A2, I find an aggregate demand elasticity that is equal to 5.56, compared to an aggregate demand elasticity of 5.64 when I do not control for the substitute portfolio.33This finding implies that markets are segmented, because if investors would treat Dutch debt as being similar to other safe European debt, then the sensitivity to changes in the Dutch yields spread should be very high (ie,╬▓0iÔåÆÔê×and henceÔêÆÔêéqit(h)Ôêépt(h)ÔåÆÔê×Ôüá).

However, these results have to be interpreted with care. The reason is that the Cragg-Donald Statistic only passes the threshold of 10 for rejecting weak instruments for the mutual fund sectorStock and Yogo 2005. The Cragg-Donald Statistic for the other sectors varies between 6.49 and 8.63, which implies that the maximal bias of the IV relative to OLS is 20% or less (rather than 10% or less when meeting the threshold of 10). I therefore leave a deeper analysis of estimating cross-elasticities across various safe European bond markets to future research.

5.4 Short-term versus long-term demand elasticities

So far, the analysis does not distinguish between short-term and long-term price elasticities of demand. However, if investors display inertia in their portfolio allocation decisions as is well-documented for the household sector (e.g.,Brunnermeier and Nagel 2008;Andersen et┬áal. 2020;Gabaix et┬áal. 2023), long-term demand elasticities will be higher than short-term demand elasticities because investors react to price changes with a lag.

To study short-term versus long-term price elasticities of demand in the demand system, I rewrite Equation (13) following Gabaix et┬áal. (2023) as: ln Ôüí B i t ( h ) = ¤ò ln Ôüí B i t A ( h ) + ( 1 ÔêÆ ¤ò ) ln Ôüí B i t I ( h ) , (14) where ln Ôüí B i t A ( h ) equals the portfolio allocation when the investor rebalances following the demand system in Equation (13) and ln Ôüí B i t I ( h ) = ln Ôüí B i t ÔêÆ 1 ( h ) the portfolio allocation when the investor does not rebalance. 34

In this setting, the long-term demand elasticity equalsÔêéqit(h)Ôêépit(h)and the short-term demand elasticity equals¤òÔêéqit(h)Ôêépit(h)Ôüá. EstimatingEquation (14)only requires to control for the lagged portfolio allocationlnÔüíBitÔêÆ1(h)inEquation (13). The coefficient for the lagged portfolio allocation equals1ÔêÆ¤òÔüá, from which we in turn can obtain¤òto estimate the short-term and long-term demand elasticities.

Table A3of the appendix summarizes the results. The short-term demand elasticities are close to the elasticities estimated in the main specification (Table 7) with an aggregate short-term price elasticity of demand of 4.87. This result is intuitive, because in the main specification I only estimate the direct response to price changes, that is, the short-term elasticity. The long-term demand elasticity is substantially higher and equals 9.26, or the short-term elasticity divided by the aggregate persistency╬©=0.5across investors. This implies that in the long run the increase in 30-year yields equals approximately 8.5 basis points, compared to the short run increase of 17 basis points.

There exists substantial heterogeneity in the persistency parameter╬©across investors. The estimates reveal that banks are least portfolio inert (Ôüá╬©=0.67Ôüá), whereas the P&I sector is most portfolio inert (Ôüá╬©=0.26Ôüá). The latter finding is not surprising because of the buy and hold strategies often implemented by this sector.

5.5 Why are banks the most price elastic?

Interestingly, as shown inFigure┬á3, banks substantially lowered their exposure to 20-year bonds after the regulatory reform while at the same time they increased their holdings of 30-year bonds, which is consistent with banks being most price elastic. The percentage of TAO held by each investor type is based on the average holdings the year prior and after the reform, however, the figures look similar regardless of the specific quarter, which shows that banks are not just temporary liquidity providers. Quantitatively, banks moved from holding 9% of the total amount outstanding of bonds with maturities close to 20 years to 0% after the regulatory reform. Likewise, they moved from holding 1% of the total amount outstanding of bonds with maturities exceeding 30 years to 18% after the reform.35This finding provides evidence that banks bought 30-year bonds from the P&I sector, while at the same time they sold 20-year bonds to the P&I sector.

This finding raises the natural question of why banks absorb demand shocks by long-term investors, and not other investor types.Figure┬á4shows that over 60% of the government bonds with maturities beyond 10 years are held by the P&I sector. This is a conservative estimate, because part of the foreign sector also consists of P&Is, such as those in the United Kingdom and Switzerland that are not part of my data set. These investors do not respond to prices, to the contrary, their (slightly) upward-sloping demand curves show that they move in the same direction as prices. As theoretically shown byDomanski, Shin, and Sushko (2017), demand for bonds by P&Is goes up when interest rates decline to match the duration of their liabilities.

Mutual funds hold another 11% of the Dutch debt outstanding. As shown inTable┬á7, their price elasticities of demand are downward sloping, but relatively small in magnitude. This implies that mutual funds are not elastic across maturity buckets. Mutual funds are typically evaluated against benchmarks (e.g.,Pavlova and Sikorskaya 2023) and the duration of those benchmarks are therefore predetermined. As such, mutual funds may experience less room to alter the maturity structure of their bond holdings.

The final major sector in government bond markets is the banking sector. Banks, on the contrary, engage in maturity transformation. Their liabilities are short-term, but they have assets which are long-term. Maturity transformation does not expose them to interest rate risk per se, because their liabilities do not move one to one with interest rates because of banksÔÇÖ market power in deposit marketsDrechsler, Savov, and Schnabl 2021. As such, a change in the maturity structure of assets does not necessarily affect bank equity negatively. Additionally, a market dominated by the P&I sector may give banks an information advantage, because they are closely connected with the P&I sector through their derivative trades. Using derivative positions of all institutional investors established in the Netherlands that are reported as part of the EMIR regulation since 2017,Table A4shows that over 85% of the derivative contracts of P&Is are with banks.36In particular, this pattern is consistent across maturities, and thus, P&Is perform the majority of their interest rate hedging activities with banks.

Furthermore,Figure┬á5shows that Dutch banks are also on the opposite side of the swap positions of P&Is. The figure shows the net exposure toward different maturity buckets, computed as the total position in receiver swaps minus the total position in payer swaps.37I then report the results separately for the P&I sector and for the banksÔÇÖ positions with the P&I sector. Interestingly, banks have the exact opposite exposures compared to the P&I sector. In particular, they have the largest negative net exposure to the 20-year interest rate, whereas the P&I sector has the largest positive net exposure to this rate.38Based on these findings, it is plausible to assume that banks have detailed information about P&IsÔÇÖ demand for specific maturities, which in turn allows them to react quickly when demand shocks occur.

article.6 Effects of the UFR at the European Lev:
In this final section, I examine the impact of the UFR implementation at the European level on bond yields in other European countries. Additionally, I analyze bond holdings data for the insurance sector in euro area countries to reveal a significant surge in demand for 20-year bonds, consistent with the patterns observed for Dutch P&Is.

6.1 Yield effects at the European level

The UFR is an important aspect of the EU Solvency II regulation, which harmonized insurance regulation across the EU, that was announced in August 2015 and took effect as of January 1, 2016. Relying on the home-bias found in euro area investorsÔÇÖ portfolios (e.g.,Koijen et┬áal. 2017,2021) and the fact that insurance companies in the Netherlands are subject to similar regulations as other countries in Europe, one may expect similar effects on the yield curve in other European countries, too.39To test for the effect of the UFR on a broader scale, I construct a panel that comprises 20 European countries subject to Solvency II regulations and regress the yield spreads of these countries on a proxy for insurance demand in those countries that is interacted with a dummy that equals one after the announcement of the UFR.40However, the results that follow should not be interpreted as causal, because by the time Solvency II was announced in August 2015, it was already evident that the UFR would be incorporated into the new regulatory framework. This is different from the Netherlands, which announced the implementation of the UFR 3 years before it was officially announced by EIOPA. Hence, anticipation effects are much less of a concern for my main analysis compared to the external validity exercise I perform here.

The following countries are included in the panel over the period from 2006 to 2020: Austria, Belgium, Bulgaria, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Ireland, Italy, Netherlands, Norway, Poland, Portugal, Slovakia, Spain, Sweden, and the United Kingdom. The size of the insurance sector differs substantially in these countriesScharfstein 2018;Greenwood and Vissing-Jorgensen 2018. Therefore, I use the size of the insurance sector in each country relative to its debt as a proxy for insurance demand for long-term bonds. The larger the size of the insurance sector relative to debt, the higher demand for long-term assets in that country. I obtain the sizes of the insurance sectors from EIOPA Insurance Statistics.41

In the regressions, I control for other variables that determine yield spreads: the 10-2 year government bond spread, the debt-to-GDP ratio, the CDS spread, and the age of the population (measured by the fraction of the elderly relative to the total population). The 10-2 year government bond spread controls for the slope of the term structureScholtens and Tol 1999. The debt-to-GDP ratio controls for the fact that countries with more debt, or a higher supply of bonds, likely have higher yields (e.g.,Greenwood and Vayanos 2014). CDS spreads control for the effects of differences in default risk across countries on yield spreads. The age of the population controls for countries with older populations that have more government debt supply and larger term spreadsGuibaud, Nosbusch, and Vayanos 2013. In some of the specifications, country fixed effects are also included to control for omitted persistent country characteristics that potentially affect yield spreads, such as differences in countriesÔÇÖ financial systems.

I then run the following regression: y c , t ( h ) ÔêÆ y c , t ( s ) = ╬▒ + ╬▓ 0 SIZEIC c 2015 ├ù UFR t + ╬▓ 1 X c , t + ╬╗ t + ╬¢ c + ¤Á c , t , (15) where y c , t ( h ) ÔêÆ y c , t ( s ) is the h minus s year government bond spread in country c at time t ; UFR t equals one as of August 2015 and zero otherwise (except for Denmark, the Netherlands, and Sweden, where the dummy equals one as of June 2012, July 2012, and February 2013, respectively); SIZEIC c 2015 is the measure of the demand for long-term bonds by the insurance sector of country c in 2015; X c , t includes country controls: 10-2 year spread, debt-to-GDP, CDS spread, and age; ╬╗ t are time fixed effects; and ╬¢ c country fixed effects.

An increase in the 30-20 year spread is consistent with the findings for the Dutch P&I sector. 6.2 Changes in bond holdings by euro area insurance companies

The simultaneous widening of the 30-20 year spread and the reduction in the 20-10 year yield spread, while the 30-10 year spread remains unchanged, suggests that the yield effects are predominantly driven by an increased demand for 20-year bonds. To verify this hypothesis, I turn to the euro area SHSS data used in the previous section which provides information on the bond holdings of the insurance sector in each euro area country.

Before considering the portfolio adjustments, I split countries in two groups. This step is crucial because, in the previous analyses, I implicitly assumed that all countries started from a similar position before the implementation of Solvency II. However, Solvency II introduced a standardized framework for insurance companies across Europe, replacing the previous national regulations. One notable difference was the substantial variation in discounting standards across insurance companies, with many euro area countries relying on historical cost valuation of their liabilities rather than mark-to-market valuation as applied in the Netherlands. As a consequence, the regulatory value of liabilities was not sensitive to market interest rates in those countries that applied historical costs. The theoretical model outlined earlier would therefore suggest that insurance companiesÔÇÖ demand for 30-year bonds was likely low to begin with. I therefore separate the countries into those that applied historical costs and those that applied mark-to-market valuation of the liabilities.

Turning to the bond portfolio allocations,Figure┬á6shows the allocation to 30-year bonds and those with maturities close to 20 years before and after the reform for those countries that applied mark-to-market valuation (panel A) versus those that applied historical costs (panel B).43An interesting pattern emerges from this figure. Initially, countries applying historical costs had significantly lower demand for both 30-year bonds and those with maturities close to 20 years. Before the reform, the average allocation to 30-year (20-year) bonds in their portfolios equaled 2% (10%), compared to 10% (15%) for those using mark-to-market valuation. This finding aligns with the notion that the regulatory hedge demand was largely insensitive to market interest rate changes.

Following the reform, countries employing mark-to-market valuation increased their 20-year bond holdings while substantially reducing their 30-year bond holdings. On the other hand, countries relying on historical costs only increased their 20-year bond holdings, with no change observed in their 30-year bond holdings. This observation reinforces the modelÔÇÖs prediction: for countries using historical costs, liabilities remained insensitive to 30-year interest rate changes under the new framework, while their sensitivity to 20-year rates increased. Security-level regressions inTable IA10 of the Internet Appendixsupport these findings, confirming that demand for 30-year bonds remained unchanged while demand for those with maturities close to 20 years increased.

To better understand the implications of the findings on yield effects, we must consider the changes in demand relative to the total amount outstanding (TAO) of debt in each country. Panels C and D ofFigure┬á6illustrate the shift in the fraction of TAO held by insurers in their respective country. That is, I calculate the total bond holdings of the insurance sector in its own countryÔÇÖs debt and express it as a percentage of the TAO of debt in that country. Interestingly, a similar pattern emerges: countries that employed mark-to-market valuation of their liabilities held a slightly higher share of the outstanding 20-year bonds, but substantially less of the 30-year bonds. On the other hand, for those that used historical costs, the fraction of outstanding 20-year bonds held by insurers increased, while their holdings of 30-year bonds remained unchanged. This further corroborates the modelÔÇÖs predictions and strengthens the link between the observed changes in demand for specific bond maturities and the resultant yield effects found earlier in this section.

InInternet Appendix IA9, I study further implications of the UFR on European yield spreads and asset allocations. In particular, I study the effect of transitionals on yield spreads, where transitionals exempted some countries from using the UFR in the short-term. Moreover, I study the change in risky asset exposures across euro area countries as a result of the implementation of the UFR.
article.7 Conclusion:
In this study, I use holdings data and price data simultaneously to study demand shifts and their causal effect on yields. In particular, I exploit a change in the regulatory discount curve at which the liabilities of long-term investors are evaluated and find a structural change in demand for long-dated assets that led to a downward pressure on 20-year yields but to an upward pressure on longer maturity yields.

Exploiting the heterogeneity in demand shifts across long-term investors shows that constrained investors reacted more heavily to the regulatory reform compared to unconstrained ones which has important implications for the vulnerability of the pension and insurance sector going forward.

My results also show that regulation plays a nontrivial role in the demand for long-term bonds which in turn, affects the yields of these bonds. This finding has direct implications for the role of regulation in determining the governmentÔÇÖs cost of borrowing.

Finally, by estimating the demand elasticities of investors in the government bond market, I show that the banking sector is most price elastic and primarily responsible for absorbing demand shocks by the P&I sector. My findings therefore suggest that regulators should take the spillover effects to other sectors into account when analyzing policies targeted at long-term investors.
